coredata.dc:title: Understanding the size effect of graphene quantum dots on protein adsorption
coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Mengdi
Shen, Qiyin
Shen, Jia-Wei
Jin, Lehong
Zhang, Li
Sun, Quan
Hu, Quan
Liang, Lijun
coredata.dc:description: Due to the unique structural and mechanical properties, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are considered as potential candidates in the field of biosensors, bioimaging, and drug delivery etc. In this work, the adsorption of protein villin headpiece (HP35) on GQDs with different sizes was investigated by molecular dynamics simulations. The simulation results identified the key role of the π-π stacking interactions between the aromatic residues of HP35 and GQDs as the binding site. More importantly, with the increase of GQD size, the amount and binding strength of adsorbed residues increase, and sequentially enhance the structure change of adsorbed protein, verified by various analysis of protein structures etc. These findings may improve the understanding of the cytotoxicity and biosafety of GQDs, and hence promote the design and application of GQDs-based biomedical devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Molecular dynamics simulation
Protein adsorption
Graphene quantum dots (GQDs)
Size effect

QJD1602011 ) and the Project Grants 521 Talents Cultivation of Zhejiang Sci-Tech University .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Graphene quantum dots with diﬀ ;erent sizes: GQD7, GQD19, GQD61, and GQD275. (b) Snapshots of initial structure of HP35 on GQDs with different sizes, including HP35-GQD7, HP35-GQD19, HP35-GQD61 and HP35-GQD275. The proteins are shown in newcartoons mode with pink helix and gray loop, and GQDs are shown in cyan.
Root-mean-square deviation (RMSD) of HP35 adsorbed on GQDs with different sizes, including (a) GQD7, (b) GQD19, (c) GQD61 and (d) GQD275.

The time evolution of the secondary structures of HP35 during its adsorption process on GQDs surface with different sizes in four representative trajectories. (a) GQD7, (b) GQD19, (c) GQD61, (d) GQD275. Here, the secondary structures are determined by the DSSP program [43].
The RMSF of HP35 absorbed on GQDs with different sizes. Data were obtained through the average of all trajectories.

(a) Representative snapshots of HP35 adsorption on GQD275 surface in third trajectory of HP35-GQD275 system. The proteins are shown in newcartoons mode with pink helix and gray loop, and the GQD275 is shown in cyan. The aromatic residues are shown in blue, and other residues are shown in yellow. (b) The minimum distance between the residues and GQD275 during the simulation (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(a) Representative snapshots of HP35 adsorption on GQD7 surface. The proteins are shown in newcartoons mode with pink helix and gray loop, and GQD7 is shown in cyan. The aromatic residues are shown in blue, and other residues are shown in yellow. (b) The minimum distance between the residues and GQD7 during the simulation. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(a) Representative snapshots of HP35 adsorption on GQD19 surface. The proteins are shown in newcartoons mode with pink helix and gray loop, and the GQD19 is shown in cyan. The aromatic residues are shown in blue, and other residues are shown in yellow. (b) The minimum distance between the residues and GQD19 during the simulation (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

(a) Representative snapshots of HP35 adsorption on GQD61 surface. The proteins are shown in Newcartoons mode with pink helix and gray loop, and the GQD61is shown in cyan. The aromatic residues are shown in blue, and other residues are shown in yellow. (b) The minimum distance between the residues and GQD61 during the simulation (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

head.ce:title: Understanding the size effect of graphene quantum dots on protein adsorption

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Medicine, Hangzhou Normal University, Hangzhou 310016, People’s Republic of China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Life Information Science and Instrument Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to the unique structural and mechanical properties, graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are considered as potential candidates in the field of biosensors, bioimaging, and drug delivery etc. In this work, the adsorption of protein villin headpiece (HP35) on GQDs with different sizes was investigated by molecular dynamics simulations. The simulation results identified the key role of the π-π stacking interactions between the aromatic residues of HP35 and GQDs as the binding site. More importantly, with the increase of GQD size, the amount and binding strength of adsorbed residues increase, and sequentially enhance the structure change of adsorbed protein, verified by various analysis of protein structures etc. These findings may improve the understanding of the cytotoxicity and biosafety of GQDs, and hence promote the design and application of GQDs-based biomedical devices.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, nanomaterials with unique structural and mechanical properties have been extensively studied. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) is a new member in the carbon nanomaterials family. Its lateral dimension is less than 100 nm and originates from graphene and graphene oxide [1]. Recently, GQDs received considerable attention for their unique morphology, ultra-small lateral sizes and exceptional electrical and mechanical properties. For example, highly oxidized GQDs with a good oxidative stress response and significantly high phototoxicity can act as a new nano-radiosensitizer in tumor radiotherapy [2]. More interestingly, GQDs showed numerous novel physical properties different from graphene due to abundant surface functionality, the quantum conﬁnement and edge effects [3]. These properties make GQDs as a promising material in biosensing, bioimaging, drug delivery systems etc. [4–9].

To realize the application of GQDs in biomedical fields, it is of great importance to evaluate and understand the cytotoxicity and biosafety of GQDs. Recently, the cytotoxicity of graphene has been extensively evaluated by both experimental and theoretical studies [10–14]. For instance, Chong et al. found that the cytotoxicity of graphene nanosheets are mediated by blood-protein coating [14]. Tian et al. reveal that graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets retard cellular migration via disruption of Actin Cytoskeleton [15]. Akhavan et al. pointed out that GO nano-walls reduced by hydrazine were more toxic to the bacteria than the unreduced graphene oxide nano-walls [16]. It is worth noting that in most works, graphene and GO were modiﬁed by proteins, polyethylene glycol (PEG), or other organic matrix for evaluations of cytotoxicity and other biological eﬀ ;ects [17–20]. For example, Zuo et al. reported that the adsorption of protein villin headpiece (HP35) onto a graphene could change the structures of protein by the π-π stacking interactions between the graphene and HP35′s aromatic residues [21]. These studies have improved the understanding on the cytotoxicity of graphene and GO, however, due to the reduction of GQDs size, the cytotoxicity of GQDs may be quite diﬀ ;erent from that of graphene and GO. By investigating the effects of GQDs on the cell viability, internal cellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) level, mitochondrial membranes potential and cell cycles, Wu et al. conclude that the cytotoxicity of GQDs is lower than that of the micrometer-sized graphene oxide (GO) [22]. Li et al. found that hydroxylated-graphene quantum dots could induce DNA damage and disrupt microtubule structure in human esophageal epithelial cells [23]. However, the toxicity profile of GQDs varied in the reports with different test subjects and GQDs with different particles size, concentration, surface chemistry and chemical doping etc., summarized by a recent review paper [24].

One of the major reasons for the cytotoxicity of nanomaterials could be attribute to the interaction between nanomaterials and biomolecules in cell. These biomolecules include cell membrane, DNA, protein etc. However, the molecule-level interaction between protein and GQDs has been rarely studied so far. In particular, the mechanism of protein adsorption on GQDs surface with different sizes and chemical modifications at the molecular level is still obscure, which limits the understanding of the cytotoxicity of GQDs. Therefore, it is of great interest to investigate the interaction between protein and GQDs with different sizes and sequentially evaluate the effect of adsorption on protein structures.

Molecular dynamics (MD) simulations have been widely applied to investigate complicated problems in the ﬁeld of bionanotechnology in recent years [25–28]. Recent MD simulations of protein onto graphene have illustrated the association between protein and graphene. For example, Gu et al. found the important role of basic residues in the adsorption of blood proteins onto the graphene surface [29]. Zhang et al. reported that graphene disrupted the interactions between two contacting proteins, indirectly disputed the cell’s functions and even lead to the cell’s mortality [30]. Fang et al. provided a fundamental understanding of the interaction mechanism of GQDs with protein through identiﬁcation and characterization of the key “binding sites” (residues), with a combined experimental and theoretical approach [31]. In this study, we investigated the adsorption process of HP35 on GQDs surface by all-atoms MD simulations. Villin headpiece (HP) is an F actin-binding domain that resides in the far C-terminal of the super villin, which is a tissue-specific acting-binding protein [21]. The HP35 protein is an independently folding three-helix bundle, and it is widely used as a model protein in the study of protein folding due to its fast folding dynamics [32,33].

All GQDs with different sizes were set in the x−y plane, as described in our previous work [34,35]. One carbon atom was set as the center atom with the Cartesian coordinates (0, 0, 0). Other carbon atoms satisfying x 2 + y 2 < R 2 were rendered as the carbon atoms in GQDs, where R is the radius of GQDs. As shown in Fig. 1 (a), the geometry of all GQDs was a circle in this study, and the index of GQDs was defined according to the number of fused benzenes. More specifically, they are GQD7, GQD19, GQD61 and GQD275, respectively, as displayed in Fig. 1(a). The edges of all GQDs were ﬁrst saturated by hydrogen atoms, then the structures of all GQDs were optimized by Gaussian 03 [36]. After that, the optimized structures were used as the initial structure of GQDs in all MD simulations. In our simulations, the native structure of HP35 was derived from the Protein Data Bank (PDB code: 1YRF [37]). HP35 has only 35 resides and is an independently folding three-helix bundle [21,32,33]. The HP35 was first equilibrated in the solution in the NPT ensemble, and the equilibrated structure was used as the initial structure for the simulation of protein adsorption on GQDs surface. Afterwards, HP35 was placed on top of the surface of GQDs with different sizes. The minimum distance between HP35 and GQDs was 0.8 nm in each system, as shown in Fig. 1(b). The TIP3P [38] water model was used for solvation in all systems, and two Cl− atoms were added into the solution to neutralize the system.

All MD simulations were performed by using GROMACS-5.0.4 package. The force field parameters for HP35 were obtained from CHARMM27. The charge of carbon atoms away from the edge were assigned as 0, while the partial charges of hydrogen atoms and the linked carbon atoms in GQDs are +0.115e and −0.115e. The parameters of GQDs including harmonic bond potentials for CH and CC bonds, harmonic angles for CCH and CCC angles, and harmonic dihedral potentials as well as Lennard-Jones (LJ) parameters were taken from Ref. [39]. These parameters have been successfully applied to investigate GQDs translocation across lipid membranes in our previous work [40]. The covalent bonds involving H atoms were constrained by LINCS algorithm [41]. All systems have experienced 50,000 steps energy minimization and 100 ps pre-equilibration first, and then for every single GQDs-protein system (eg. GQD7-HP35 complex in solution), 4 independent production runs with 500 ns each were carried out. The only difference in 4 trajectories for the same system is the initial velocity distribution, because the initial velocity of atoms in the system in every run was generated by a pseudo random seed. In all production runs, NPT ensemble was used with a constant temperature of 310 K imposed by v-rescale thermostat and 1 bar of pressure controlled by Berendsen barostat. The time step was set as 2 fs and the cutoﬀ ; for the nonbonded van der Waals interaction was set as 1.2 nm. The long range electrostatic interaction was calculated by particle mesh Ewald (PME) summation, with a cutoﬀ ; of 1.2 nm for the separation of the direct and reciprocal space summation. The simulation results were analyzed by various GROMACS postprocessing routines. Visualization of MD trajectories were implemented by VMD [42].

In our simulations, all trajectories showed that protein HP35 could spontaneously adsorbed on the surface of GQDs with different sizes in various degree. This result is consisting with the previous simulation and experiment work [31]. To illustrate the conformational changes of HP35 in detail, the root-mean-square deviation (RMSD) of HP35 during the adsorption process (shown in Fig. 2 ) in all systems was calculated. Fig. 2 showed that the RMSD of HP35 in four systems experienced various fluctuations and converges to 0.16 nm, 0.24 nm, 0.24 nm and 0.36 nm for these systems (average over last 100 ns in all independent trajectories). The RMSD of HP35 in the system HP35-GQD275 is largest and far different from other three systems. As shown in Fig. 2(d), the RMSD of the third trajectory increased from 0.2 nm to 0.78 nm within 47 ns, and the RMSD of the fourth trajectory increased from 0.2 nm to 0.56 nm within 183 ns, which changed more greatly compared with other systems. To further investigate the structural changes of HP35, the radius of gyration (Rg) of HP35 during adsorption on GQDs with different sizes was also calculated (see Fig. S1 in Support Information). It was found that the results were consist with the RMSD of HP35 in four systems, and the Rg of HP35 in the third and fourth trajectory of HP35-GQD275 changed most obviously.

The secondary structural changes of HP35 in four systems during MD simulation were analyzed to verify the results of RMSD and Rg of HP35. It was found that in two of four trajectories, the secondary structure of the protein has greatly changed after adsorption on the GQD275 surface comparing with that on GQDs with smaller size (GQD7, GQD19, GQD61), as shown in Fig. 3 . In a representative trajectory (trajectory 3) of HP35-GQD275 system, part of the third α-helix converted into a 310-helix, turn and coil, while the ﬁrst α-helix partially converted to turn, as shown in Fig. 3(d). However, the secondary structural changes of HP35 in other three protein-GQDs systems were relatively small, as shown in Fig. 3(a), (b) and 3(c). From the RMSD, Rg and the secondary structure analysis, one could figure out that the size of GQDs plays an important role in protein adsorption on GQDs’ surface, and with the increase of the GQDs size, the structure of the protein changed more obviously after protein adsorption.

To investigate the structure changes of individual residues, the root-mean-square fluctuation (RMSF) of HP35 after the protein adsorption on the surface of GQDs with different sizes were calculated, as shown in Fig. 4 . These data were obtained through the average of all trajectories. One could find that the fluctuation of the residues located in two ends of HP35 is highest in all systems. The aromatic residues, such as F10, W23 and F35 experience more structure change, due to relative strong π-π stacking interaction between their conjugated rings and GQDs. More importantly, as shown in Fig. 4, the fluctuation of the RMSF of HP35 on GQD275 is more obvious than that in other three systems, especially for the structure located in two ends and residue F10.

During the adsorption process, many residues of the HP35 protein, particularly those aromatic ones were lying flat on the GQDs surface due to the strong π-π stacking interactions. To illustrate the adsorption dynamics of these residues, the third trajectory of GQD275 was chosen as the representative trajectory for the analysis. Fig. 5 (a) displayed some representative snapshots at different simulation times in this trajectory. As shown in Fig. 5(a), at t = 0, HP35 and GQD275 were initially separated. At t = 16 ns, the aromatic residue F35, which was located at the end of the third α-helix, formed a ﬂat binding mode with GQD275 (shown in Fig. 5(a)). The distance between the residue F35 and GQD275 was 0.36 nm (shown in Fig. 5(b)). Residue F35 binds with the GQD275 due to the π-π stacking interaction, which was a strong interaction between graphitic material and the aromatic residues [21]. Meanwhile, the RMSD increased from 0.2 nm to 0.4 nm (see Fig. 2(d)) during this period, which led to a spatial rearrangement of the three α-helices of HP35. At t = 47 ns, the residues W23, an aromatic residue on the other end of the third α-helix, formed the ﬂat binding mode with GQD275. The distance between the residue W23 and the GQD275 were 0.36 nm (shown in Fig. 5(b)). Thus, with strong adsorption of residues F35 and W23 on GQD275 surface, the third α-helix was finally adsorbed on the GQD275 surface, which led to secondary structure changes of the protein. During this process, the RMSD of HP35 increased from 0.2 nm to 0.78 nm (as shown in Fig. 2(d)), and part of the third α-helix converted into turn and coil (see Fig. S1(d)). At t = 57 ns, the residue F10 formed the same ﬂat binding mode with the GQD275, and then the second α-helix was adsorbed on the GQD275 surface (shown in Fig. 5(a)) and the distance between the residue F10 and the GQD275 were also 0.36 nm. These distances are roughly consisting with the distance between aromatic rings and graphitic material, which is around 0.40 nm [21]. During t = 57∼500 ns, the fluctuations of the RMSD is relatively small, which indicate relative mild structure change of HP35.

To better understand the size effect of GQDs on protein adsorption, we also checked the secondary structural changes in other three systems. As shown in Fig. 6 (a), we chose the fourth trajectory of HP35-GQD7 as an example. At t = 0, HP35 and GQD7 were initially separated. At t = 26 ns, the residue L1 were absorbed on the GQD7 surface, and the distance between the residue L1 and GQD7 were around 0.32 nm (shown in Fig. 6(b)). At t = 62 ns, the aromatic residue F10 was absorbed on the GQD7 surface by π-π interaction with GQD7, and the distance between the residue

F10 and GQD7 were decrease to 0.34 nm. However, at t = 95 ns, HP35 was desorbed from GQD7 surface and there were no residues absorbed. At t = 122 ns, the aromatic residue F35 formed the same ﬂat binding mode with GQD7, and the distance between residue F35 and GQD7 return to 0.32 nm (shown in Fig. 6(b)). Meanwhile, L1 and F10 were adsorbed on to the surface and kept the adsorption states during the rest simulation time. The strong adsorption of terminal L1 residue is quite similar to the result of Fang et al., where L71 in the C-terminal of ubiquitin was found to have very high NMR chemical shift perturbation (CSP) values [31]. This probably could attribute to the strong hydrophobic interaction between the alkyl sidechain of L1 and GQD7. However, F35 always in the adsorption/desorption equilibration during 500 ns MD simulation, mainly due to relative small binding energy on small size GQD7 and its relative large flexibility. According to Fig. 3(a), the secondary structural changes of HP35 in this system is quite small and the RMSD of HP35 fluctuated mildly (shown in Fig. 2(a)).

Then the first trajectory of HP35-GQD19 system was analyzed. HP35 and GQD19 were initially separated at t = 0 ns. At t = 11 ns, the aromatic residue W23 were absorbed on GQD19 surface by π-π interaction and ﬂat binding mode with GQD19 (see Fig. 7 (a)), and the distance between residue W23 and GQD19 was 0.35 nm (shown in Fig. 7(b)). However, the adsorption of W23 is relatively weak and it constantly in the equilibration of adsorption/desorption. For example, at t = 146 ns, none of the residues locate within 0.5 nm of GQD19, which indicate weak adsorption of HP35 on GQD19 surface. At the end of 500 ns simulation, residue W23 were not absorbed on GQD19 surface, and the distance between the residue W23 and GQD19 were about 1.04 nm (shown in Fig. 7(b)). The RMSF of residue W23 in HP35-GQD19 system was highest, as show in Fig. 5. This may reflect the high flexibility of W23 during the adsorption. Therefore, the interactions between HP35 and GQD19 mainly through identiﬁcation and characterization of surface binding of the key residue W23. Meanwhile, at the end of MD simulation, L1 and F10 have the minimum distance of 1.57 nm and 0.75 nm, which further imply relatively weak adsorption of HP35 on GQD19 surface compare with that on GQD275 surface.

As shown in Fig. 8 , we chose the secondary trajectory of HP35-GQD61 as an example to analyze. The adsorption of HP35 on GQD61 can be described simply as three stages. In the first stage from 0 to 3 ns, the residue W23 and Q26 were absorbed on GQD61 (shown in Fig. 8(a)), and the distance between residue W23 and GQD61 decreased to 0.34 nm, while the distance between residue Q26 and GQD61 decrease to 0.33 nm (seen Fig. 8(b)). In the second stage from 3 to 179 ns, the residue W23 and Q26 maintained the adsorption state and the distance between these two residues and GQD61 were less than 0.4 nm. In the third stage from 180 to 500 ns, the residue F35 were absorbed on GQD61 surface, and the minimum distance between residue F35 and GQD61 were 0.37 nm. Above all, we found that there are three residues (W23, Q26, F35) absorbed on GQD61 at the end of 500 ns simulation, which was consisted with the peak location of residue RMSF in HP35-GQD61 system (shown in Fig. 4). In this system, the number of adsorbed residues is higher than that in HP35-GQD7 and HP35-GQD19 systems, and the secondary structure change, especially in the region of third α-helix of HP35, is more obvious than that in HP35-GQD7 and HP35-GQD19 systems. Combining with the results of HP35-GQD275 system we discussed above, these findings indicate that with the increase of GQD size, the amount and strength of adsorbed residues increase, and hence increase the structure change of adsorbed protein.

Recently, there is a review paper concluded that size is a key factor for the low toxicity feature of GQDs. Although size can induce significant difference among the toxicities of graphene oxide (GO), carbon nanotube (CNT) and GQDs, they haven’t yet found a study which provides direct evidence for unveiling how the size of GQDs changes their toxicity profile [24]. In this work, the size effect of GQDs on protein adsorption were studied and discussed. We found that with increase of GQD size, the structure change of the studied protein (HP35) increased. In particular, GQD275 with largest size in this study induced largest structure deformation of HP35. As the biological function of protein highly related to the protein structure, larger deformation of protein structure could decrease the biological function of protein and thus increase the cytotoxicity of GQDs.

In this study, MD simulation was employed to investigate the adsorption of HP35 on GQDs with different sizes, and the size effect of GQDs on protein adsorption was discussed. It was found that protein HP35 could spontaneously adsorbed on the surface of GQDs with different sizes in various degree. Generally, aromatic residues and residues with alkyl sidechain have potentials of binding strongly to the GQD surface. The aromatic residues interact with the GQD surface mainly through the π-π stacking interaction, while residues with alkyl sidechain bind to GQD surface through hydrophobic interaction. More importantly, with the increase of GQD size, the amount and binding strength of adsorbed residues increase, and sequentially enhance the structure change of adsorbed protein, proved by the analysis of RMSD, RMSF and secondary structures etc. As the biological function highly relate to the structure of protein, our study may improve the understanding of the cytotoxicity and biosafety of GQDs, and promote the design and application of GQDs-based biomedical devices.
The authors declare no competing ﬁnancial interest.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge financial support by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant Nos. 21674032, 21503186), Qianjiang Talents Program of Zhejiang Province (Nos. QJD1602011) and the Project Grants 521 Talents Cultivation of Zhejiang Sci-Tech University.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2018.11.059.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

Graphene oxide nanosheets retard cellular migration via disruption of actin cytoskeleton

Using graphene to protect DNA from cleavage during cellular delivery

Protein corona mitigates the cytotoxicity of graphene oxide by reducing its physical interaction with cell membrane
Heparin-modified graphene oxide loading anti-cancer drug and growth factor with heat stability, long-term release property and lower cytotoxicity
Adsorption of Villin Headpiece onto graphene, carbon nanotube, and C60: effect of contacting surface curvatures on binding affinity
Insight into the cellular internalization and cytotoxicity of graphene quantum dots
Hydroxylated-graphene quantum dots induce DNA damage and disrupt microtubule structure in human esophageal epithelial cells

Improved heat spreading performance of functionalized graphene in microelectronic device application
Molecular modelling of translocation of biomolecules in carbon nanotubes: method, mechanism and application
Charge-tunable insertion process of carbon nanotubes into DNA nanotubes
DNA sequencing by two-dimensional materials: As theoretical modeling meets experiments
The role of basic residues in the adsorption of blood proteins onto the graphene surface
Potential toxicity of graphene to cell functions via disrupting protein-protein interactions
Understanding the graphene quantum dots-ubiquitin interaction by identifying the interaction sites
Pathways to a protein folding intermediate observed in a 1-microsecond simulation in aqueous solution
Atomic-level characterization of the structural dynamics of proteins

Simulation of activation free energies in molecular systems

LINCS: a linear constraint solver for molecular simulations
